summary: Implementation plan for the 1985-NY-Yankees Azure Function: a nightly Timer-triggered Python 3.11 function that queries GPT-4o via TRAPI, validates the 1985 New York Yankees roster response, and persists it to dedicated Azure Blob Storage using Managed Identity for all service authentication. The plan covers IaC provisioning, TRAPI integration, blob write logic, CI/CD pipeline, observability, security hardening, and release readiness.
assumptions: TRAPI auth and endpoint are escalated to the TRAPI team; explicit response deadline is 2026-04-22T17:00:00Z (owner: rciapala).
APP-01 may start only after TRAPI auth scope, base URL, and API version are confirmed in writing by the deadline owner.
Player scope is limited to the 25-man active roster (24–28 range accounts for roster edge cases).
TRAPI is accessible over the public internet; no VNet integration required for initial deployment.
Consumption Plan is approved for production; cold-start risk at 2 AM UTC is acceptable for this workload.
Staging will use a separate Function App (not deployment slots) because slots are not available on Consumption Plan.
GitHub Actions OIDC federated identity is used for Azure deployments (no long-lived service principal secrets).
Existing infra/ Bicep modules (storage, functionapp, rbac, monitoring, alerts) will be extended, not replaced.
The host.json functionTimeout will be set to 120 seconds; maxConcurrentCalls = 1.
Application Insights sampling is enabled at the SDK level to control ingestion cost.
open_questions: Escalated on 2026-04-18: TRAPI auth mechanism (Managed Identity bearer token vs client credential/API key) must be confirmed by 2026-04-22T17:00:00Z (owner: rciapala).
Escalated on 2026-04-18: TRAPI base URL and GPT-4o API version/model deployment must be confirmed by 2026-04-22T17:00:00Z (owner: rciapala).
Resolved on 2026-04-18: scope is active roster only (24-28 players); coaching/front-office/IL are out of scope.
Is TRAPI network-restricted (requiring VNet integration and private endpoint for storage)?
Resolved on 2026-04-18: use Consumption Plan for production and a separate staging Function App instead of slots.

waves.name: Foundation
waves.objective: Resolve external blockers, confirm TRAPI access, provision all Azure infrastructure, and establish CI/CD skeleton so all other work can proceed in parallel.
entry_criteria: spec.md reviewed and open questions triaged
Azure subscription and resource group available
GitHub Actions OIDC credentials configured for the repository
exit_criteria: TRAPI endpoint, model version, and auth scope confirmed in writing
All Azure resources provisioned via Bicep (Function App, dedicated storage, Key Vault, App Insights, RBAC)
CI/CD pipeline deploys a stub function to staging slot successfully

waves.name: Core Build
waves.objective: Implement the TRAPI client, response validator, blob writer, and Timer Trigger function; wire prompt template and host configuration.
entry_criteria: TRAPI credentials/scope available in Key Vault or confirmed as Managed Identity scope
Dedicated storage account provisioned with yankees-roster container
exit_criteria: GetAndStoreYankeesRoster function executes end-to-end locally and in staging
Unit tests pass for TRAPI client, validator, and blob writer modules
Prompt template pinned and committed to prompts/get_1985_yankees.txt

waves.name: Hardening
waves.objective: Add reliability controls (retry/backoff, failure path, conditional PUT), observability (structured logging, custom metrics, alerts), and security hardening (Key Vault references, least-privilege RBAC review).
entry_criteria: Core Build wave exit criteria met
Function successfully writes valid roster blob in staging
exit_criteria: Retry logic with exponential backoff verified by unit tests
Failed-response path writes to failed/ prefix and triggers Application Insights alert
All three alert rules active in staging: failure count, duration, data quality metric
No hardcoded secrets; all sensitive config via Key Vault references
Integration test confirms Mattingly, Winfield, Henderson present in blob output

waves.name: Release Readiness
waves.objective: Validate end-to-end in staging, complete documentation, perform staged production rollout via slot swap, and confirm monitoring is active.
entry_criteria: All unit and integration tests passing in CI
exit_criteria: Staging smoke test passes (manual trigger + blob verified)
Production slot swap completed via GitHub Actions
Monitoring alerts confirmed active in production
README documents local dev setup, TRAPI auth, and blob naming convention
Nightly execution verified in production on first scheduled run

tasks.title: Review and approve implementation task plan
tasks.description: Review this task plan for completeness, sequencing, and alignment with spec.md requirements. Resolve open questions (especially TRAPI auth and endpoint) before approving. Dependency graph is included below.
outputs: Approved task plan
Resolved TRAPI auth/endpoint open questions documented
acceptance_criteria: All tasks reviewed and estimated by owner
TRAPI auth mechanism and endpoint confirmed or escalated with explicit deadline
Player scope (active roster only vs. broader) confirmed
Consumption Plan vs. Premium Plan decision documented
Dependency graph reviewed and wave sequencing approved
risks: TRAPI auth uncertainty can block APP-01 and APP-02 indefinitely if not resolved early
Deployment slot availability on Consumption Plan may require infra rework

PM-01 (no deps)
  └── INF-01 (no deps)
        ├── INF-02 (depends: INF-01)

        │           └── SEC-01 (depends: INF-01, INF-02, INF-03)
        │                 └── APP-01 (depends: INF-01, INF-02, INF-03, SEC-01)
        │                       ├── APP-02 (depends: APP-01)
        │                       │     └── APP-03 (depends: APP-01, APP-02)
        │                       │           └── APP-04 (depends: APP-01, APP-02, APP-03)
        │                       │                 ├── OPS-01 (depends: APP-04, INF-03)
        │                       │                 ├── REL-01 (depends: APP-01, APP-02, APP-03)
        │                       │                 ├── QA-01 (depends: APP-01, APP-02, APP-03, APP-04)
        │                       │                 │     └── QA-02 (depends: QA-01, APP-04)

tasks.approval_notes: Task plan reviewed for completeness, sequencing, and spec alignment. TRAPI auth/endpoint items escalated with explicit deadline; player scope and hosting plan decisions documented.

estimate_review.notes: All task estimates reviewed and accepted for initial execution.

hosting_plan.decision: Consumption Plan for production; separate staging Function App in place of deployment slots.

tasks.title: Provision core Azure infrastructure via Bicep
tasks.description: Extend existing Bicep modules to provision the dedicated storage account (Standard_LRS, Cool tier, soft-delete 7 days, yankees-roster container private), Function App (Consumption Plan, Python 3.11, system-assigned Managed Identity), Log Analytics workspace, workspace-based Application Insights (30-day retention, sampling enabled), and Azure Key Vault (zone-redundant). Wire APPLICATIONINSIGHTS_CONNECTION_STRING and AzureWebJobsStorage__accountName into Function App settings.
inputs: spec.md architecture and resource requirements sections
outputs: Updated infra/main.bicep and modules (storage, functionapp, monitoring, keyvault)
All resources provisioned in target resource group
acceptance_criteria: Dedicated storage account provisioned with Standard_LRS, Cool access tier, and allowSharedKeyAccess:false
yankees-roster container created with publicAccess:None and soft-delete retention of 7 days
Function App provisioned on Consumption Plan with Python 3.11, system-assigned Managed Identity enabled
Key Vault provisioned zone-redundant with RBAC authorization model
Application Insights connected to Function App with 30-day retention and sampling enabled
Bicep deployment completes with no errors via az deployment group create
risks: Consumption Plan does not support deployment slots — may require a separate staging Function App
allowSharedKeyAccess:false requires all SDK calls to use identity-based auth; ensure SDK version compatibility

tasks.title: Provision RBAC role assignments via Bicep
tasks.description: Add Bicep role assignment resources granting the Function App Managed Identity: Storage Blob Data Contributor scoped to the yankees-roster container, Key Vault Secrets User on the Key Vault, and Storage Blob Data Owner on the host storage account (for AzureWebJobsStorage identity auth). All assignments must be scoped to minimum required resource, not subscription or account level.
inputs: INF-01 outputs (resource IDs for Function App identity, storage account, Key Vault)
outputs: infra/modules/rbac.bicep updated with all required role assignments
Role assignments verified in Azure portal
acceptance_criteria: Storage Blob Data Contributor assigned to Managed Identity scoped to yankees-roster container (not account-level)
Key Vault Secrets User assigned to Managed Identity on the Key Vault
Storage Blob Data Owner and Queue Data Contributor assigned for host storage AzureWebJobsStorage identity auth
No wildcard or subscription-scope role assignments
Role assignments idempotent on re-deployment (no duplicate assignment errors)
risks: Container-scoped role assignment may not be supported in all Bicep/ARM versions — verify scope syntax
Missing Queue Data Contributor will cause Function App host to fail on startup

tasks.title: Provision staging environment infrastructure
tasks.description: Provision a staging Function App (or deployment slot if Consumption Plan supports it) with identical configuration to production but pointing to a separate staging storage container or account. Wire all App Settings and Key Vault references identically to production. If Consumption Plan does not support slots, provision a second Function App resource for staging.
inputs: PM-01 decision on Consumption Plan vs. Premium Plan for slots
outputs: Staging Function App or slot provisioned and accessible
Staging environment App Settings configured
acceptance_criteria: Staging Function App (or slot) deployed with Python 3.11 and Managed Identity enabled
Staging App Settings mirror production (TRAPI endpoint, Key Vault references, storage account name)
Staging environment connects to Application Insights (same workspace, separate cloud role name)
Staging deployment verified by deploying stub function and confirming execution in App Insights
risks: Consumption Plan slot limitation may require Premium Plan or a second Function App, increasing cost

tasks.title: Configure Key Vault secrets and App Settings references
tasks.description: Store TRAPI credentials (endpoint URL, auth scope or API key, model deployment name) in Azure Key Vault. Configure Function App App Settings to reference Key Vault secrets via @Microsoft.KeyVault(SecretUri=...) references. Ensure no secrets are committed to source control or appear in plain text in any config file.
inputs: INF-01 and INF-02 outputs (Key Vault provisioned, Managed Identity RBAC assigned)
TRAPI auth mechanism confirmed via PM-01
outputs: TRAPI secrets stored in Key Vault
Function App App Settings using Key Vault references
local.settings.json.example updated with placeholder variable names (no values)
acceptance_criteria: TRAPI endpoint URL, auth scope, and model version stored as Key Vault secrets
Function App App Settings reference Key Vault secrets (not plain text values)
Key Vault reference resolution verified: az functionapp config appsettings list shows resolved values
No secrets present in any committed file (confirmed by git log and grep scan)
local.settings.json.example lists all required env var names with placeholder values only
risks: Key Vault reference resolution requires Managed Identity RBAC to be fully propagated (can take minutes)
If TRAPI uses non-standard auth, Key Vault secret structure may need adjustment

tasks.title: Establish GitHub Actions CI pipeline with test gate
tasks.description: Create a GitHub Actions workflow that triggers on pull requests and pushes to main: installs Python 3.11 dependencies from requirements.txt, runs pytest with coverage for all modules under tests/, and fails the build if any test fails. Use OIDC federated identity (not long-lived secrets) for any Azure calls.
outputs: .github/workflows/ci.yml with lint, test, and coverage steps
CI badge in README
acceptance_criteria: GitHub Actions workflow triggers on PR and push to main
pytest runs all tests in tests/ with --cov report
Build fails if any test fails or coverage drops below threshold (set threshold after QA-01)
No long-lived service principal secrets stored in GitHub secrets (OIDC only)
CI run completes successfully on a stub commit
risks: OIDC federated identity setup requires Azure AD app registration — confirm permissions

tasks.title: Build GitHub Actions CD pipeline with staging slot swap
tasks.description: Create a GitHub Actions CD workflow triggered on push to main (after CI passes): deploys the function package to the staging slot/app, runs a smoke test (manual trigger via Azure Functions admin API + blob existence check), then swaps staging to production. Uses OIDC federated identity for all Azure CLI calls.
inputs: Azure Functions Core Tools or zip deploy method
outputs: .github/workflows/cd.yml with deploy-staging, smoke-test, and slot-swap jobs
acceptance_criteria: CD workflow deploys to staging on push to main after CI passes
Smoke test job triggers the function manually and asserts a blob was written in staging storage
Slot swap (or equivalent staging-to-production promotion) only occurs after smoke test passes
Rollback path documented: re-swap or re-deploy previous artifact on failure
Deployment workflow uses OIDC (no long-lived credentials in GitHub secrets)
risks: Consumption Plan slot swap not supported — requires alternative promotion strategy (separate app deploy)
Smoke test timing: function may take >30s on cold start, requiring retry logic in smoke test step

tasks.title: Implement TRAPI client module with retry and timeout
tasks.description: Implement trapi_client.py (or extend existing) to: authenticate via DefaultAzureCredential with the TRAPI-specific scope, load and send the pinned prompt from prompts/get_1985_yankees.txt, enforce a 45-second HTTP timeout, and apply 3-attempt exponential backoff retry on transient failures (5xx, timeout). Pin the GPT-4o model version in the request payload. Return the raw JSON response body.
inputs: Confirmed TRAPI endpoint URL, auth scope, model deployment name (from SEC-01/PM-01)
outputs: Updated trapi_client.py with timeout, retry, and prompt-loading logic
prompts/get_1985_yankees.txt committed to source control
acceptance_criteria: TRAPI client reads prompt from prompts/get_1985_yankees.txt (not hardcoded string)
GPT-4o model version is pinned in the request payload (read from App Settings)
HTTP call enforces 45-second timeout via httpx or requests timeout parameter
3 retries with exponential backoff applied on 5xx responses and timeout errors
DefaultAzureCredential used with TRAPI-specific scope (no hardcoded credentials)
Unit tests cover: successful response, timeout retry, 3-retry exhaustion raises exception
risks: TRAPI auth scope unknown until PM-01 resolved — blocks implementation
Model version pinning requires TRAPI to support model deployment name parameter

tasks.title: Implement response validator module
tasks.description: Implement a validator module that parses the TRAPI JSON response against the expected schema ({players: [{name, position, jersey_number}]}), asserts player count is in range [24, 28], and raises a structured ValidationError on failure. The validator should be pure (no I/O) to enable easy unit testing.
inputs: spec.md Requirement 1 response schema and acceptance criteria
outputs: src/validator.py (or equivalent) with schema validation and count-range check
Unit tests in tests/test_validator.py
acceptance_criteria: Validates JSON structure matches {players: [{name: str, position: str, jersey_number: int}]}
Raises ValidationError if player count < 24 or > 28
Returns validated player list on success
Unit tests cover: valid response, missing fields, wrong types, count below/above range
Validator is pure function (no side effects, no I/O dependencies)
risks: GPT-4o may return slightly different field names or types on prompt drift — validator must be tested with real TRAPI responses

tasks.title: Implement blob writer module with conditional PUT
tasks.description: Implement or extend blob_writer.py to: write validated roster JSON to yankees-roster/{run_date_utc}.json using DefaultAzureCredential (Managed Identity), use If-None-Match: * conditional PUT to prevent duplicate writes on same-day retrigger, and write raw failed responses to yankees-roster/failed/{run_date_utc}.json on validation failure.
inputs: spec.md Requirement 2 blob naming and storage spec
INF-01 dedicated storage account name (from App Settings)
outputs: Updated blob_writer.py with conditional PUT and failed/ path logic
Unit tests in tests/test_blob_writer.py
acceptance_criteria: Blob name is {run_date_utc}.json (UTC date, not local time)
Conditional PUT uses If-None-Match: * header — raises ResourceExistsError on duplicate without overwriting
Failed/invalid responses written to yankees-roster/failed/{run_date_utc}.json
DefaultAzureCredential used (no connection string or shared key)
Storage account name read from App Settings (not hardcoded)
Unit tests cover: successful write, duplicate write idempotency, failed blob write path
risks: azure-storage-blob SDK conditional PUT behavior must be tested against real storage — mock tests may not catch header handling issues

tasks.title: Implement Timer Trigger function and host configuration
tasks.description: Implement the GetAndStoreYankeesRoster Azure Function in function_app.py using the v2 programming model Timer Trigger with schedule '0 0 2 * * *', useMonitor=True, and explicit UTC timezone. Wire TRAPI client, validator, and blob writer calls in sequence. Emit structured log events and the player_count_returned custom metric. Set host.json functionTimeout=120, maxConcurrentCalls=1.
outputs: Updated function_app.py with Timer Trigger and full orchestration logic
Updated host.json with functionTimeout and maxConcurrentCalls
prompts/ directory in repo root
acceptance_criteria: Timer Trigger schedule is '0 0 2 * * *' with useMonitor=True
Function completes end-to-end locally using func start and a manual trigger
Structured log events emitted: function_started, trapi_request_sent, trapi_response_received, blob_write_succeeded, function_completed
player_count_returned custom metric emitted via Application Insights SDK on each successful run
host.json sets functionTimeout to 120 and extensions.http.maxConcurrentRequests (or timer maxConcurrentCalls) to 1
Function handles validation failure path: writes to failed/ and logs structured error before exiting
risks: Azure Functions v2 programming model useMonitor parameter syntax differs from v1 — verify with SDK docs
Custom metric emission via OpenTelemetry SDK requires correct instrumentation key wiring

tasks.title: Implement retry, backoff, and failure-path alerting
tasks.description: Harden the TRAPI client with tenacity or equivalent library for 3-attempt exponential backoff (base 2s, max 30s). Implement the validation failure path end-to-end: catch ValidationError in function_app.py, write raw response to failed/ blob prefix, emit a custom Application Insights exception event, and re-raise so the function execution is marked as failed (triggering the execution failure alert).
inputs: spec.md Reliability NFR and Alerting section
outputs: Hardened trapi_client.py with tenacity retry decorator
Validated failure path in function_app.py
Unit tests for retry exhaustion and failure path
acceptance_criteria: tenacity (or equivalent) retry applied with 3 attempts, exponential backoff, jitter
Retry only on transient errors (5xx, timeout) — not on 4xx or ValidationError
On retry exhaustion, exception propagates to function_app.py and is logged as structured error
Validation failure writes raw response to failed/ prefix before re-raising
Unit tests confirm retry count, backoff intervals (mocked), and failure blob write
risks: Retry on TRAPI calls may push total execution time beyond 60s target — monitor duration in staging

tasks.title: Provision Application Insights alert rules via Bicep
tasks.description: Extend infra/modules/alerts.bicep to add three alert rules: (1) execution failure count > 0 in 1-hour window → email rciapala, (2) function duration > 90 seconds → email rciapala, (3) custom metric player_count_returned < 24 or > 40 → email rciapala. Use existing Action Group or create one.
outputs: Updated alerts.bicep with three alert rules
Alert rules deployed and visible in Azure Monitor
acceptance_criteria: Execution failure alert fires when function exceptions > 0 in 1-hour window
Duration alert fires when avg execution duration > 90 seconds
Data quality alert fires when player_count_returned custom metric < 24 or > 40
All alerts notify rciapala via email Action Group
Alert rules deployed via Bicep (not manually configured)
Alerts tested in staging by intentionally triggering each condition
risks: Custom metric player_count_returned must be emitted by the function before the alert can be validated
Scheduled query alert for custom metrics may have up to 5-minute evaluation delay

tasks.title: Write and validate unit test suite
tasks.description: Write comprehensive unit tests covering: TRAPI client (success, timeout, retry exhaustion), response validator (valid/invalid schema, count range boundaries), blob writer (successful write, duplicate idempotency, failed/ path), and function orchestration (success path, validation failure path). Target ≥80% coverage on all application modules.
outputs: tests/test_trapi_client.py, tests/test_validator.py, tests/test_blob_writer.py, tests/test_function_app.py
Coverage report ≥80%
acceptance_criteria: pytest runs all tests with zero failures
Coverage ≥80% on trapi_client, validator, blob_writer, and function_app modules
Known player assertions tested: Mattingly, Winfield, Henderson present in parsed output
Retry logic tested with mocked HTTP responses (3 failures then success; 3 failures then exception)
Conditional PUT idempotency tested with mocked ResourceExistsError
All tests runnable locally via python -m pytest tests/ -v --cov
risks: Mocking DefaultAzureCredential and azure-storage-blob SDK requires careful fixture design
Integration-level assertions on known players require a real or recorded TRAPI response fixture

tasks.title: Execute end-to-end integration test in staging
tasks.description: Manually trigger GetAndStoreYankeesRoster in the staging environment, verify the roster blob is written to yankees-roster/{today_utc}.json in the staging storage account, confirm Mattingly/Winfield/Henderson appear in the output JSON, and verify Application Insights shows the player_count_returned metric and all structured log events.
inputs: OPS-01 alert rules active in staging
outputs: Integration test result documented (pass/fail with blob URL and App Insights screenshot)
Known player assertions confirmed
acceptance_criteria: Roster blob written to staging storage at yankees-roster/{run_date_utc}.json
JSON blob contains 24–28 players including Mattingly, Winfield, and Henderson
player_count_returned custom metric visible in Application Insights within 5 minutes
All five structured log events visible in Application Insights traces
Duplicate trigger on same day does not overwrite existing blob (conditional PUT verified)
Validation failure path tested: inject invalid TRAPI response, confirm failed/ blob written and alert fires
risks: TRAPI availability in staging environment may differ from production
Application Insights ingestion latency may delay metric/log visibility

tasks.title: Write README and operational runbook
tasks.description: Update README.md to include: local dev setup (prerequisites, func start instructions, local.settings.json.example walkthrough), TRAPI auth instructions (how to obtain credentials, Key Vault secret names), blob naming convention, and how to manually trigger the function. Include a brief operational runbook section covering alert response procedures for each of the three alert rules.
outputs: Updated README.md with local dev, TRAPI auth, blob naming, and runbook sections
acceptance_criteria: README includes step-by-step local dev setup (clone, pip install, set env vars, func start)
TRAPI auth section documents Key Vault secret names and how to populate them
Blob naming convention documented with example (yankees-roster/2026-03-31.json)
Operational runbook covers response actions for all three Application Insights alerts
README reviewed and approved by rciapala
risks: TRAPI auth instructions depend on PM-01 resolution — may need a placeholder until confirmed

tasks.title: Execute production deployment and verify monitoring
tasks.description: Trigger the GitHub Actions CD pipeline to deploy to production via staging slot swap (or equivalent). Confirm the first nightly execution at 2 AM UTC succeeds, the roster blob is written to the production storage account, and all three Application Insights alert rules are active. Document the production deployment in the release log.
inputs: OPS-01 alert rules deployed to production
outputs: Production Function App running GetAndStoreYankeesRoster on nightly schedule
First production blob verified
All monitoring alerts active
acceptance_criteria: CD pipeline deploys to production via staging promotion without manual steps
First nightly execution at 2 AM UTC writes roster blob to production storage
Roster blob contains 24–28 players including Mattingly, Winfield, Henderson
All three Application Insights alert rules confirmed active in production (Azure Monitor)
No hardcoded secrets in any deployed configuration (verified via App Settings inspection)
Deployment documented with timestamp, artifact version, and approval sign-off
risks: First scheduled production run may fail if cold start exceeds function timeout — monitor closely
Slot swap promotion not available on Consumption Plan — alternative promotion must be validated in CD pipeline
